publication.changeSummary: Misconception MCQ, canonical fill-gap, and no code task for References, heap and garbage collection; every item remains blocked from formal exams pending independent human review.

items.title: References, heap and garbage collection: reject the misconception
items.prompt: Which statement is source-aligned for Java: Beginner to Intermediate and corrects this misconception? Java cannot leak memory because garbage collection frees every object that the application no longer intends to use.

examEligibility.rationale: AI-assisted draft item; independent human source, technical, pedagogy, accessibility, and answer-oracle review is required before formal-exam eligibility.

hints: Compare each option with both the language-specific boundary case and the cited operational model.

feedback.correct: Correct. Garbage collection reclaims only unreachable objects on its own schedule; diagnose logical retention by tracing reachability from roots and release non-memory resources explicitly.
feedback.incorrect: That choice preserves the misconception. Garbage collection reclaims only unreachable objects on its own schedule; diagnose logical retention by tracing reachability from roots and release non-memory resources explicitly.

criteria.description: Selects the source-aligned correction and rejects the documented misconception.

privateAuthorNotes: A human reviewer must verify source fidelity and ensure the distractor remains plausible without teaching it as fact.

options.text: Garbage collection reclaims only unreachable objects on its own schedule; diagnose logical retention by tracing reachability from roots and release non-memory resources explicitly.
Java cannot leak memory because garbage collection frees every object that the application no longer intends to use.

answer.explanation: Garbage collection reclaims only unreachable objects on its own schedule; diagnose logical retention by tracing reachability from roots and release non-memory resources explicitly.
